  • Hide All Code

Disease Progression Model in Diabetes

Umesh Singh

2025-12-27

Disease Progression Model

Project

Aims of this project was to develop a disease progression model for diabetes. In this project, the different states are defined according to the glycosylated hemoglobin levels: - State 1: Normoglycemia (HbA1c<5.7)
- State 2: Pre-Diabetes (HbA1c 5.7-6.4)
- States 3: Diabetes (HbA1c>6.4)

The attending practitioner monitor the status of subjects with diabetes periodically over time. The patient comes to the clinic a random number of times and at random times. Relevant covariate information is recorded at every visit. During a clinic visit, the glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level is assessed. The status of the subject is determined by the HbA1c level.
Research Question:
- Given a specific state of the patient at the current visit, what will be her/his state at the next visit (months, years) and
- how will that state be influenced by her/his gender, race and Risk score.

Cohort Building

Data

# detach("package:digest", unload = TRUE)
# install.packages("digest")
# packageVersion("digest")
library(digest)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(msm) # multistate modeling
library(plyr) # extract info from data
# options(repos="https://CRAN.R-project.org")
# install.packages("openxlsx")
# library(openxlsx)
remove(dpm)
dpm<-read_excel("E:\\ARC\\Projects\\Dis Prog Model\\dpm\\DPM1.xlsx")
dpm$datevisit <- as.Date(dpm$datevisit, format = "%m/%d/%Y" )
dpm$DOB <- as.Date(dpm$DOB, format = "%m/%d/%Y" )
dpm$X <- as.Date('2025-12-27')
dpm$LastVisit=(dpm$X-dpm$datevisit)

# dpm$ID_hash <- sapply(dpm$ID, function(x) digest(x, algo = "sha256", key = salt))
salt <- Sys.getenv("HASH_SALT")
dpm$ID_hashed <- sapply(dpm$ID,  function(x) digest(paste0(x, salt), "sha256"))

dpm$ID_hash <- substr(dpm$ID_hashed,
                      nchar(dpm$ID_hashed) - 5,
                      nchar(dpm$ID_hashed))

dpm %>% select(-c(ID,DOB,ID_hashed)) %>% head()

The project is based on data from 242 unique patients with a total of 726 visits. Relevant data were accessed from the EHR of the high-risk patients for diabetic mellitus who are enrolld at the Center. Deidentified data was used in the following analysis.

  • Describing the Dataset
    • ID_hash = Patient ID (hashed)
    • age_years = age of the patient in years exact to the day of visit
    • gender1 = gender (0 = female; 1= male )
    • status = state of diabetes (1= no diabetes; 2=prediabetes; 3=diabetes)
    • cumint = cumulative duration in months from the first visit. Set month = 0 on the day of first visit
    • int_mo = months after last visit at the center.
    • visitnum = Visit number of the specific subject
    • datevisit= date of each visit
    • DOB = date of birth (not shown)
    • A1C = glycosylated hemoglobin level (marker of diabetes status)
    • race1 = race of the patient
  • critical feature of the data:
      1. A patient visits at random times.
      1. A patient visits random number of times.
      1. Total length of time between visits is also random.

Research Question

Let’s assume: The physician has a patient currently on a check-up visit.
- The state for the patient now is pre-diabetes.
- What are the chances s/he will have diabetes
+ in a month? or
+ a year?
- How this probability is modulated by her/his race and gender or risk score.

Data Dimensions

dim(dpm)
## [1] 722  17
  • Total of 722 observations and 14 variables

Exploratory Analysis

visits by patient

DPM<-ddply(dpm, "ID_hash", summarise, Visits=length(ID))
mean(DPM$Visits)
## [1] 2.995851
sd(DPM$Visits)
## [1] 1.042824
  • An average of 3 visits per patient

max/worse state by patient

Finalstate<-ddply(dpm, "ID_hash", summarise, Final=max(status))
head(Finalstate)
  • 1 = normal 2 = pre-diabetes 3 = diabetes

max/worse state counts:

Finalstate<-ddply(dpm, "ID_hash", summarise, Final=max(status))
table(Finalstate$Final)
## 
##   1   2   3 
##  12  37 192
  • 12 subjects remained normoglycemic, 37 subjects became / remained pre-diabetic from normoglycemic / prediabetic states, 193 subjects had diabetes at one-point at least

Empirical Analysis

one-step transitions: counts

library(msm)
Trans1<-statetable.msm(status, ID_hash, data=dpm)
rownames(Trans1) <- c("From Normal", "From Pre-diabetes", "From Diabetes")
colnames(Trans1) <-c("To Normal", "To Pre-diabetes", "To Diabetes")
Trans1
##                    to
## from                To Normal To Pre-diabetes To Diabetes
##   From Normal              32              15           4
##   From Pre-diabetes         4              74          45
##   From Diabetes            17              42         248
  • Does not accounts for predictors: Count one-step transitions …What was the stage at the next visit
  • The table shows the one-step transition counts for all the 242 patients in the dataset
  • In all there were 484 transitions. The topleft to downright diagonal and middle column is dominant.

one-step transitions: Sankey diagram

library(highcharter)

sankey_links <- expand.grid(
  from_label = rownames(Trans1),
  to_label = colnames(Trans1),
  KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = FALSE,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    weight = as.vector(Trans1),
    from = dplyr::recode(
      from_label,
      `From Normal` = "current_normal",
      `From Pre-diabetes` = "current_prediabetes",
      `From Diabetes` = "current_diabetes"
    ),
    to = dplyr::recode(
      to_label,
      `To Normal` = "next_normal",
      `To Pre-diabetes` = "next_prediabetes",
      `To Diabetes` = "next_diabetes"
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(weight > 0) %>%
  dplyr::select(from, to, weight)

sankey_nodes <- data.frame(
  id = c(
    "current_normal", "current_prediabetes", "current_diabetes",
    "next_normal", "next_prediabetes", "next_diabetes"
  ),
  name = c(
    "Current: Normal", "Current: Pre-diabetes", "Current: Diabetes",
    "Next: Normal", "Next: Pre-diabetes", "Next: Diabetes"
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
  highcharter::highchart() %>%
    highcharter::hc_title(text = "Observed One-Step Patient Flow") %>%
    highcharter::hc_subtitle(text = "Empirical transitions from the current visit state to the next visit state") %>%
    highcharter::hc_add_series(
      data = sankey_links,
      nodes = sankey_nodes,
      type = "sankey",
      hcaes(from = from, to = to, weight = weight),
      name = "Observed transitions"
    ) %>%
    highcharter::hc_tooltip(
      pointFormat = "{point.fromNode.name} to {point.toNode.name}: <b>{point.weight}</b> transitions"
    ) %>%
    highcharter::hc_plotOptions(
      series = list(
        dataLabels = list(enabled = TRUE, format = "{point.name}")
      ),
      sankey = list(
        nodeWidth = 24,
        curveFactor = 0.5
      )
    )
} else {
  knitr::kable(
    sankey_links,
    col.names = c("From", "To", "Observed transitions"),
    caption = "Observed one-step transitions used in the Sankey diagram."
  )
}
  • The Sankey diagram visualizes observed patient flow from the current state to the next recorded state.
  • It is based on empirical one-step transition counts and does not account for predictors or time between visits.

one-step transitions: percentages

Trans2<-100*round(prop.table(Trans1,1),3)
Trans2
##                    to
## from                To Normal To Pre-diabetes To Diabetes
##   From Normal            62.7            29.4         7.8
##   From Pre-diabetes       3.3            60.2        36.6
##   From Diabetes           5.5            13.7        80.8
  • If the patient is in Normoglycemic State currently, the chances that s/he will remain Normoglycemia are 62%.
  • If the patient is in Pre-diabetic State currently, the chances that s/he will remain Pre-diabetic state are 60%.
  • If the patient is in Diabetic State currently, the chances that s/he will remain ““Diabetec** are 80.5%.
  • Note Empirical analysis does not take into account predictors and time.

Model Building

Fit a Markov process model

Initial Generator for Iteration
Q <- rbind(c(0, 0.25, 0.5), c(0.166, 0, 0.5), c(0.16, 0.25, 0) )
Q
##       [,1] [,2] [,3]
## [1,] 0.000 0.25  0.5
## [2,] 0.166 0.00  0.5
## [3,] 0.160 0.25  0.0

Initializing the Q matrix. The diagonals set at zero. R-program figures out the actual values.

Specifying initial values
Q.crude<-crudeinits.msm(status~cumint, ID_hash, data = dpm, qmatrix = Q)
Q.crude
##            [,1]       [,2]       [,3]
## [1,] -1.3790018  1.0886856  0.2903162
## [2,]  0.1134597 -1.3898819  1.2764221
## [3,]  0.2113708  0.5222101 -0.7335809
  • to start searching for maximum; no covariates are entertained at this time.
  • The analysis is purely based on data with state and duration (months) since last visit. The matrix provides rates of decay needed for estimating transition probability matrices.*
Results: Estimates
dpm.msm <- msm(status ~ cumint, subject = ID_hash, data= dpm, qmatrix = Q, deathexact = FALSE)
dpm.msm
## 
## Call:
## msm(formula = status ~ cumint, subject = ID_hash, data = dpm,     qmatrix = Q, deathexact = FALSE)
## 
## Maximum likelihood estimates
## 
## Transition intensities
##                   Baseline                  
## State 1 - State 1 -1.7657 (-2.80443,-1.1118)
## State 1 - State 2  1.5957 ( 0.96386, 2.6417)
## State 1 - State 3  0.1700 ( 0.02317, 1.2479)
## State 2 - State 1  0.1161 ( 0.01517, 0.8876)
## State 2 - State 2 -2.2316 (-3.03413,-1.6413)
## State 2 - State 3  2.1155 ( 1.53501, 2.9156)
## State 3 - State 1  0.2951 ( 0.17326, 0.5027)
## State 3 - State 2  0.7716 ( 0.53472, 1.1134)
## State 3 - State 3 -1.0667 (-1.42053,-0.8010)
## 
## -2 * log-likelihood:  662.0027
  • From the estimated intensities, we see that prediabetic patients (State 2) are 18 times as likely to progress to diabetes (transitions from State 2 to State 3, vs from State 2 to State 1).

Model

# pmatrix.msm(dpm.msm, t=.1)
pmatrix.msm(dpm.msm, t=1.0)
##            State 1   State 2   State 3
## State 1 0.22841682 0.3349335 0.4366497
## State 2 0.09360756 0.3131560 0.5932364
## State 3 0.10802131 0.2686038 0.6233749
  • Producing the maximum likelihood estimate of the infinitesimal generator Q.
    • Estimate one-step transition probability matrix for any given time. What is the conditional probability of becoming diabetic in one year’s time given that the patient is currently in prediabetic state: 0.59

Add Covariates

Covariate: Risk Score RAF_RS
tidy_qmatrix <- function(qmatrix_obj) {
  q_estimate <- if (is.list(qmatrix_obj) && !is.null(qmatrix_obj$estimate)) {
    qmatrix_obj$estimate
  } else {
    qmatrix_obj
  }

  as.data.frame(as.table(as.matrix(q_estimate)), responseName = "intensity") %>%
    dplyr::rename(from_state_raw = Var1, to_state_raw = Var2) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      from_id = as.integer(from_state_raw),
      to_id = as.integer(to_state_raw)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(from_id != to_id) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      from_state = dplyr::recode(
        as.character(from_state_raw),
        `1` = "Normoglycemia",
        `State 1` = "Normoglycemia",
        `2` = "Pre-diabetes",
        `State 2` = "Pre-diabetes",
        `3` = "Diabetes",
        `State 3` = "Diabetes",
        .default = as.character(from_state_raw)
      ),
      to_state = dplyr::recode(
        as.character(to_state_raw),
        `1` = "Normoglycemia",
        `State 1` = "Normoglycemia",
        `2` = "Pre-diabetes",
        `State 2` = "Pre-diabetes",
        `3` = "Diabetes",
        `State 3` = "Diabetes",
        .default = as.character(to_state_raw)
      ),
      transition = paste(from_state, "->", to_state)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(from_id, to_id, transition, intensity)
}

dpmraf.msm<-msm(status~cumint, subject=ID_hash, data=dpm, qmatrix=Q, deathexact = FALSE, covariates=~RAF_RS)

low_risk_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmraf.msm, covariates = list(RAF_RS = 0.3), ci = "none")
high_risk_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmraf.msm, covariates = list(RAF_RS = 1.5), ci = "none")

raf_effect_tbl <- tidy_qmatrix(low_risk_q) %>%
  dplyr::rename(`RAF_RS = 0.3` = intensity) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tidy_qmatrix(high_risk_q) %>%
      dplyr::rename(`RAF_RS = 1.5` = intensity),
    by = c("from_id", "to_id", "transition")
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    `1.5 / 0.3 rate ratio` = `RAF_RS = 1.5` / `RAF_RS = 0.3`
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(from_id, to_id) %>%
  dplyr::select(transition, `RAF_RS = 0.3`, `RAF_RS = 1.5`, `1.5 / 0.3 rate ratio`)

gt::gt(raf_effect_tbl) %>%
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Effect of Risk Score on Transition Intensities",
    subtitle = "RAF_RS = 0.3 versus RAF_RS = 1.5"
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_label(
    transition = "Transition",
    `RAF_RS = 0.3` = "RAF_RS = 0.3",
    `RAF_RS = 1.5` = "RAF_RS = 1.5",
    `1.5 / 0.3 rate ratio` = "1.5 / 0.3 HR"
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Estimated intensity",
    columns = c(`RAF_RS = 0.3`, `RAF_RS = 1.5`)
  ) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = c(`RAF_RS = 0.3`, `RAF_RS = 1.5`), decimals = 3) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `1.5 / 0.3 rate ratio`, decimals = 2) %>%
  gt::cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = c(`RAF_RS = 0.3`, `RAF_RS = 1.5`, `1.5 / 0.3 rate ratio`)
  ) %>%
  gt::opt_row_striping() %>%
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states."
  )
Effect of Risk Score on Transition Intensities
RAF_RS = 0.3 versus RAF_RS = 1.5
Transition
Estimated intensity
 1.5 / 0.3 HR
RAF_RS = 0.3 RAF_RS = 1.5
Normoglycemia -> Pre-diabetes 1.579 1.655 1.05
Normoglycemia -> Diabetes 0.043 0.835 19.33
Pre-diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.201 0.027 0.14
Pre-diabetes -> Diabetes 1.963 2.408 1.23
Diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.284 0.333 1.17
Diabetes -> Pre-diabetes 0.649 1.023 1.57
Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states.
  • The table summarizes the off-diagonal transition intensities at two illustrative RAF_RS values. A 1.5 / 0.3 HR greater than 1 indicates faster transition rates at the higher risk score.
Model with constrained covariate effects
Effect of Race (baseline=AA;)
#dpmrace.msm<-msm(status~cumint, subject=ID_hash, data=dpm, qmatrix=Q, deathexact = FALSE, covariates=list("2-3"=~race1, "2-1"=~race1))
dpmrace.msm<-msm(status~cumint, subject=ID_hash, data=dpm, qmatrix=Q, deathexact = FALSE, covariates=~race1, constraint = list(race1=c(1,2,3,1,2,3)))

aa_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmrace.msm, covariates = list(race1=1), ci = "none")
non_aa_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmrace.msm, covariates = list(race1=0), ci = "none")

race_effect_tbl <- tidy_qmatrix(aa_q) %>%
  dplyr::rename(AA = intensity) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tidy_qmatrix(non_aa_q) %>%
      dplyr::rename(`Non-AA` = intensity),
    by = c("from_id", "to_id", "transition")
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    `Non-AA/AA rate ratio` = `Non-AA` / AA
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(from_id, to_id) %>%
  dplyr::select(transition, AA, `Non-AA`, `Non-AA/AA rate ratio`)

gt::gt(race_effect_tbl) %>%
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Effect of Race on Transition Intensities",
    subtitle = "AA (race1 = 1) versus non-AA (race1 = 0)"
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_label(
    transition = "Transition",
    AA = "AA",
    `Non-AA` = "Non-AA",
    `Non-AA/AA rate ratio` = "Non-AA/AA HR"
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Estimated intensity",
    columns = c(AA, `Non-AA`)
  ) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = c(AA, `Non-AA`), decimals = 3) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `Non-AA/AA rate ratio`, decimals = 2) %>%
  gt::cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(AA, `Non-AA`, `Non-AA/AA rate ratio`)) %>%
  gt::opt_row_striping() %>%
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states."
  )
Effect of Race on Transition Intensities
AA (race1 = 1) versus non-AA (race1 = 0)
Transition
Estimated intensity
 Non-AA/AA HR
AA Non-AA
Normoglycemia -> Pre-diabetes 1.581 1.591 1.01
Normoglycemia -> Diabetes 0.133 0.222 1.67
Pre-diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.072 0.145 2.01
Pre-diabetes -> Diabetes 2.133 2.147 1.01
Diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.221 0.370 1.67
Diabetes -> Pre-diabetes 0.521 1.045 2.01
Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states.

The table summarizes the off-diagonal transition intensities by race. A Non-AA/AA HR greater than 1 indicates faster transition rates in the non-AA group.

Effect of Gender (F vs. M)
dpmgender.msm<-msm(status~cumint, subject=ID_hash, data=dpm, qmatrix=Q, deathexact = FALSE, covariates=~gender1, constraint = list(gender1=c(1,2,3,1,2,3)))

female_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmgender.msm, covariates = list(gender1=0), ci = "none")
male_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmgender.msm, covariates = list(gender1=1), ci = "none")

gender_effect_tbl <- tidy_qmatrix(female_q) %>%
  dplyr::rename(Female = intensity) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tidy_qmatrix(male_q) %>%
      dplyr::rename(Male = intensity),
    by = c("from_id", "to_id", "transition")
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    `Male/Female rate ratio` = Male / Female
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(from_id, to_id) %>%
  dplyr::select(transition, Female, Male, `Male/Female rate ratio`)

gt::gt(gender_effect_tbl) %>%
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Effect of Gender on Transition Intensities",
    subtitle = "Female (gender1 = 0) versus Male (gender1 = 1)"
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_label(
    transition = "Transition",
    Female = "Female",
    Male = "Male",
    `Male/Female rate ratio` = "Male/Female HR"
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Estimated intensity",
    columns = c(Female, Male)
  ) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = c(Female, Male), decimals = 3) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `Male/Female rate ratio`, decimals = 2) %>%
  gt::cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(Female, Male, `Male/Female rate ratio`)) %>%
  gt::opt_row_striping() %>%
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states."
  )
Effect of Gender on Transition Intensities
Female (gender1 = 0) versus Male (gender1 = 1)
Transition
Estimated intensity
 Male/Female HR
Female Male
Normoglycemia -> Pre-diabetes 1.507 1.741 1.15
Normoglycemia -> Diabetes 0.205 0.103 0.50
Pre-diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.119 0.120 1.01
Pre-diabetes -> Diabetes 1.994 2.302 1.15
Diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.356 0.179 0.50
Diabetes -> Pre-diabetes 0.769 0.774 1.01
Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states.

The table summarizes the off-diagonal transition intensities by sex. A Male/Female HR greater than 1 indicates faster transition rates among males.

Effect of Gender (F vs. M) & Race (baseline=AA)
dpmgenderrace.msm <-msm(status~cumint, subject=ID_hash, data=dpm, qmatrix=Q, deathexact = FALSE, 
                        covariates=~gender1 + race1, constraint = list(gender1=c(1,2,3,1,2,3)))

female_aa_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmgenderrace.msm, covariates = list(gender1=0, race1=1), ci = "none")
male_non_aa_q <- qmatrix.msm(dpmgenderrace.msm, covariates = list(gender1=1, race1=0), ci = "none")

genderrace_effect_tbl <- tidy_qmatrix(female_aa_q) %>%
  dplyr::rename(`Female, AA` = intensity) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tidy_qmatrix(male_non_aa_q) %>%
      dplyr::rename(`Male, non-AA` = intensity),
    by = c("from_id", "to_id", "transition")
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    `Male non-AA/Female AA rate ratio` = `Male, non-AA` / `Female, AA`
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(from_id, to_id) %>%
  dplyr::select(transition, `Female, AA`, `Male, non-AA`, `Male non-AA/Female AA rate ratio`)

gt::gt(genderrace_effect_tbl) %>%
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Effect of Gender and Race on Transition Intensities",
    subtitle = "Female, AA (gender1 = 0, race1 = 1) versus Male, non-AA (gender1 = 1, race1 = 0)"
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_label(
    transition = "Transition",
    `Female, AA` = "Female, AA",
    `Male, non-AA` = "Male, non-AA",
    `Male non-AA/Female AA rate ratio` = "Male non-AA/Female AA HR"
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Estimated intensity",
    columns = c(`Female, AA`, `Male, non-AA`)
  ) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = c(`Female, AA`, `Male, non-AA`), decimals = 3) %>%
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `Male non-AA/Female AA rate ratio`, decimals = 2) %>%
  gt::cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = c(`Female, AA`, `Male, non-AA`, `Male non-AA/Female AA rate ratio`)
  ) %>%
  gt::opt_row_striping() %>%
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states."
  )
Effect of Gender and Race on Transition Intensities
Female, AA (gender1 = 0, race1 = 1) versus Male, non-AA (gender1 = 1, race1 = 0)
Transition
Estimated intensity
 Male non-AA/Female AA HR
Female, AA Male, non-AA
Normoglycemia -> Pre-diabetes 1.679 1.470 0.88
Normoglycemia -> Diabetes 0.000 0.260 42,261.45
Pre-diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.042 0.213 5.01
Pre-diabetes -> Diabetes 1.863 2.496 1.34
Diabetes -> Normoglycemia 0.316 0.181 0.57
Diabetes -> Pre-diabetes 0.459 1.269 2.77
Off-diagonal entries from qmatrix.msm(); larger values indicate faster transitions between states.

The table compares the off-diagonal transition intensities for the two covariate profiles used in the model call. A ratio greater than 1 indicates faster transition rates in the Male, non-AA profile.
